title: Neurology — USMLE Step 1 & 2
description: Stroke, seizures, headache, dementia, movement disorders, neuromuscular disease, and CNS anatomy. High-yield for USMLE.
subject: medicine

cards.back: tPA (alteplase) within 3–4.5 hours of symptom onset; no hemorrhage on CT; BP < 185/110 mmHg
Contralateral hemiparesis/hemisensory loss (face and arm > leg), contralateral homonymous hemianopia, aphasia (dominant hemisphere) or neglect (non-dominant)
Contralateral leg > arm weakness, urinary incontinence, abulia (frontal lobe involvement)
Contralateral homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing, alexia without agraphia (dominant)

cards.front: Wallenberg (lateral medullary) syndrome: artery and findings
cards.back: PICA (posterior inferior cerebellar artery). Ipsilateral facial pain/temperature loss + ipsilateral Horner + contralateral body pain/temperature loss + dysarthria/dysphagia/vertigo

cards.front: What is the most common cause of subarachnoid hemorrhage?
cards.back: Ruptured berry (saccular) aneurysm (~85%). Classic: sudden-onset 'thunderclap headache' — worst headache of life

cards.front: Epidural hematoma: artery and CT appearance
cards.back: Middle meningeal artery (temporal bone fracture). Lucid interval + rapid deterioration. CT: biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense collection

cards.front: Subdural hematoma: vein and CT appearance
cards.back: Bridging veins (elderly, alcoholics, minor trauma). CT: crescent-shaped collection that crosses suture lines

cards.front: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure: first-line acute treatment
cards.back: Benzodiazepines (lorazepam IV or diazepam rectal). If no IV access: IM midazolam
Continuous seizure > 5 minutes OR ≥ 2 seizures without full recovery of consciousness between them

cards.front: Absence (petit mal) seizures: EEG finding and drug
cards.back: 3 Hz spike-and-wave complexes. Treatment: ethosuximide (or valproate). Do NOT use carbamazepine (worsens absence)
Most common focal epilepsy. Aura (déjà vu, olfactory hallucination), automatisms (lip-smacking, hand-wringing), post-ictal confusion
Triptans (sumatriptan) — 5-HT1B/1D agonists; NSAIDs; anti-emetics. Avoid in basilar/hemiplegic migraine
Propranolol (β-blocker), valproate, topiramate, amitriptyline, CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab)

cards.front: Cluster headache: features and acute treatment
cards.back: Male > female, unilateral periorbital pain + autonomic features (ptosis, rhinorrhea, lacrimation). Acute: 100% O2 + sumatriptan SC
Wet, Wacky, Wobbly — urinary incontinence, dementia, magnetic gait ataxia. Treatment: VP shunt
Beta-amyloid plaques (extracellular) + neurofibrillary tangles (tau protein, intracellular). Atrophy of hippocampus first
Fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism. Extreme neuroleptic sensitivity (antipsychotics can be fatal)
Autosomal dominant CAG repeat expansion in HTT gene. Striatum (caudate) degeneration → chorea, dementia, psychiatric symptoms. Anticipation

cards.front: Parkinson's disease: pathology and dopamine pathway
cards.back: Loss of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra pars compacta → decreased dopamine in nigrostriatal pathway. Lewy bodies (α-synuclein)
Tremor (resting, pill-rolling), Rigidity (cogwheel), Akinesia/Bradykinesia, Postural instability
Levodopa/carbidopa (most effective). Carbidopa inhibits peripheral DOPA decarboxylase → reduces side effects
Essential: action/postural tremor, improves with alcohol, family history. Parkinson: resting tremor, pill-rolling, improves with movement
Dissemination in space (multiple CNS lesions) AND dissemination in time (lesions at different time points) on MRI
Optic neuritis (painful unilateral vision loss, relative afferent pupillary defect — Marcus Gunn pupil)
Oligoclonal IgG bands, mildly elevated protein, increased IgG index. Normal glucose
Combined UMN + LMN signs (hyperreflexia + weakness/wasting/fasciculations). Spares sensation and eye movements. No effective cure; riluzole slows progression
Autoantibodies against nicotinic AChR at neuromuscular junction. Edrophonium (Tensilon) test: brief improvement. Treat with pyridostigmine

cards.front: Myasthenia gravis vs Lambert-Eaton: distinguishing features
cards.back: MG: worsens with use, improves with rest, ocular/bulbar involvement. L-E: improves with repetitive stimulation, proximal limb, associated with SCLC
Post-infectious (Campylobacter, CMV, EBV) ascending demyelinating polyneuropathy. CSF: albuminocytologic dissociation (↑ protein, normal WBC)
IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) or plasmapheresis. NOT corticosteroids (worsen outcomes)
Lancinating unilateral facial pain along CN V distribution triggered by light touch. Treatment: carbamazepine (first-line)
Unilateral LMN facial nerve (CN VII) palsy — affects forehead (UMN lesion spares forehead). Treatment: prednisone (+ acyclovir if HSV suspected)

cards.front: UMN vs LMN lesion: facial nerve
cards.back: UMN lesion (above facial nucleus): contralateral lower face weakness only (forehead spared — bilateral cortical input). LMN lesion: ipsilateral entire face
Unilateral sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, unsteadiness. At cerebellopontine angle. Bilateral = NF2
Ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis due to interruption of sympathetic chain. Causes: Pancoast tumor, carotid dissection, brainstem stroke
Cape-like bilateral loss of pain and temperature in upper extremities (anterior commissure fibers) with preserved proprioception/vibration (posterior columns intact)
Tertiary syphilis. Demyelination of dorsal columns + dorsal roots → loss of proprioception/vibration, Romberg sign, Charcot joints, Argyll Robertson pupils
'Prostitute pupils' — accommodate but do not react. Accommodation reflex intact, light reflex lost. Classic in neurosyphilis
Lesion in medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF). Ipsilateral medial rectus palsy (fails to adduct) + contralateral nystagmus. Most common cause: MS (bilateral INO = pathognomonic for MS)
Hemisection of spinal cord: ipsilateral UMN signs + ipsilateral loss of proprioception/vibration + contralateral loss of pain/temperature (2 levels below)

cards.front: Meningitis: CSF findings in bacterial vs viral
cards.back: Bacterial: ↑ WBC (PMNs), ↑ protein, ↓ glucose. Viral: ↑ WBC (lymphocytes), normal/↑ protein, normal glucose

cards.front: Most common bacterial meningitis organisms by age (adults 18–50)
cards.back: Neisseria meningitidis and Streptococcus pneumoniae

cards.front: Empiric treatment for bacterial meningitis in adults
cards.back: Ceftriaxone + vancomycin + dexamethasone (reduces inflammation/hearing loss). Add ampicillin if Listeria risk (elderly, immunocompromised)
Confusion + ophthalmoplegia + ataxia. Due to thiamine (B1) deficiency (alcoholism). Treatment: IV thiamine BEFORE glucose
Chronic thiamine deficiency → mammillary body damage. Anterograde > retrograde amnesia + confabulation. Often irreversible

cards.front: Peripheral neuropathy: most common cause worldwide
cards.back: Diabetes mellitus (diabetic peripheral neuropathy — length-dependent, starts distally, 'stocking-glove')
Most common hereditary peripheral neuropathy. Demyelinating CMT1A: PMP22 gene duplication. Foot drop, stocking-glove sensory loss, high arches (pes cavus)
Autosomal recessive GAA repeat (frataxin gene). Gait ataxia, loss of proprioception, cardiomyopathy (most common cause of death), diabetes, pes cavus, scoliosis
Young obese women; headache, papilledema, diplopia (CN VI palsy). Normal brain MRI, elevated opening pressure on LP. Treatment: acetazolamide, weight loss
